name: Iron
name_origin: The English name comes from Anglo-Saxon 'iren' (Old English 'isern'), of Celtic origin. The chemical symbol Fe derives from the Latin 'ferrum', meaning iron.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Iron is the fourth most abundant element in Earth's crust (~5.6% by mass) and the most abundant element by mass in the planet as a whole, forming much of the molten outer core and solid inner core. It is the sixth most abundant element in the universe and the heaviest element produced in significant quantities by stellar nucleosynthesis.
states: essential biological cofactor in hemoglobin and many enzymes

compounds.name: Iron(III) oxide (hematite)
compounds.summary: A red-brown oxide that is the principal ore of iron and the main component of common rust.

compounds.name: Iron(II,III) oxide (magnetite)
compounds.summary: A naturally magnetic black oxide that is an important iron ore and is used in magnetic recording and as a pigment.

compounds.name: Iron disulfide (pyrite)
compounds.summary: Brass-yellow 'fool's gold'; a major source of sulfur for sulfuric acid manufacture.

compounds.name: Iron(III) chloride
compounds.summary: A dark-brown deliquescent solid used in water treatment, etching printed circuit boards, and as a Lewis-acid catalyst.

compounds.name: Iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate
compounds.summary: A blue-green crystalline salt (copperas) used as a dietary iron supplement, water treatment coagulant, and ink ingredient.

compounds.name: Iron pentacarbonyl
compounds.summary: A volatile yellow liquid used as a precursor to high-purity iron powder and carbonyl iron.

uses: Production of steel and cast iron - the backbone of modern construction, machinery, and transportation
Hemoglobin in red blood cells, where iron binds and transports oxygen
Permanent magnets and electromagnets, electrical transformers, and motor cores
Catalyst in the Haber-Bosch process for ammonia synthesis
Magnetite and hematite as pigments and magnetic recording media
Iron(II) sulfate as a dietary supplement to treat anemia
reactivity_summary: Iron is a moderately reactive transition metal that readily oxidizes in moist air to form hydrated iron(III) oxide (rust), which does not protect the underlying metal. It dissolves in dilute non-oxidizing acids to give iron(II) salts and hydrogen and reacts with steam at red heat to give Fe3O4 and hydrogen. Iron exhibits two principal oxidation states, +2 (ferrous) and +3 (ferric), and exhibits ferromagnetism below 1043 K (the Curie point).
isotopes_summary: Iron has four stable isotopes: 54Fe, 56Fe (most abundant at ~91.75%), 57Fe, and 58Fe. 56Fe has the highest binding energy per nucleon of any commonly occurring nuclide, marking the practical end of energy-releasing stellar fusion. The radioisotope 60Fe (half-life 2.6 million years) is used in astrophysical and geochronological studies.
